messages.content:
A sample that does not provide a good representation of the population from which it was collected is referred to as a(n) ________ sample.

Select one:

a.
Biased


b.
Unbiased
a. Biased

The Central Limit Theorem is important in statistics because

Select one:

a.
for a large n, it says the population is approximately normal.


b.
for any sized sample, it says the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal.


c.
for any population, it says the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal, regardless of the sample size.


d.
for a large n, it says the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the population.
d. for a large n, it says the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the population.

According to the empirical rule, if the data form a "bell-shaped" normal distribution, ________ percent of the observations will be contained within 2 standard deviations around the arithmetic mean.

Select one:

a.
approximately 68%


b.
approximately 95%


c.
approximately 93%


d.
approximately 89%
b. approximately 95%

Sampling distributions describe the distribution of

Select one:

a.
population parameters.


b.
sample statistics.


c.
both population parameters and sample statistics.


d.
neither population parameters nor sample statistics.
b. sample statistics.

True or False: As the sample size increases, the standard error of the mean increases.

Select one:
True
False
False.

The standard error of the mean is population standard deviation divided by square root of sample size.

Select one:
True
False
False. The standard error of the mean is sample standard deviation divided by square root of sample size.

The standard error of the mean will be reduced when sample size increases.

Select one:
True
False
True.

The standard error of the mean measures the variability of the sample means.

Select one:
True
False
True.

the sampling distribution of the sample mean is approximately normal whenever the sample size is sufficiently large (n ≥ 30).

Select one:
True
False
True.

The mean of the sampling distribution of the sample mean is always equal to μ.

Select one:
True
False
True.

The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is always σ.

Select one:
True
False
False. The standard deviation of the sampling distribution is σ / sqrt(n).

The shape of the sampling distribution is always approximately normal.

Select one:
True
False

False. The shape of the sampling distribution is approximately normal when the sample size is large (n ≥ 30) or, for some distributions, when the sample size is smaller but the distribution is not strongly skewed.

Why is the Central Limit Theorem so important to the study of sampling distributions?


a.
It allows us to disregard the size of the sample selected when the population is not normal.


b.
It allows us to disregard the shape of the sampling distribution when the size of the population is large.


c.
It allows us to disregard the size of the population we are sampling from.


d.
It allows us to disregard the shape of the population when n is large.
d. It allows us to disregard the shape of the population when n is large.
what is private saving

Private saving refers to the portion of income that is not used for consumption expenses, but instead, is set aside for future use. It is the savings that individuals or households make, either as a precautionary measure or for meeting future expenses or investments. Private saving is an important component of the national savings of a country, and it contributes to the growth of an economy.
what is national saving

National saving refers to the total amount of savings generated in an economy over a particular time period, usually a year. It is the sum of private saving and government saving, and it represents the amount of resources available for investment in the economy. National saving is an important macroeconomic variable that affects economic growth and financial stability. When national saving is high, an economy has more resources available for investment in physical capital, human capital, and technological innovation, which can lead to higher productivity, output, and living standards in the long run.
what else can be said about national saving

In addition to its importance for economic growth and stability, national saving is also related to other macroeconomic variables such as the trade balance and public debt. 

The trade balance, which is the difference between a country's exports and imports, is affected by national saving because it determines the amount of resources available for domestic investment and production. If a country has a high national saving rate, it can use these savings to finance domestic investment and reduce its reliance on foreign borrowing, which can help to improve the trade balance.

National saving is also related to public debt because a portion of government saving is used to finance public investment and pay down public debt. If the government is able to generate a budget surplus, it can use this surplus to reduce public debt, which can help to reduce interest rates and stimulate private investment.

Overall, national saving is an important macroeconomic variable that affects many aspects of an economy, and policymakers often seek to implement policies that encourage greater levels of saving to promote stronger economic growth and financial stability.
